article.title: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) in Tobacco: Isolation of Activated Genes Suggests Role of the Phenylpropanoid Pathway in Inhibition of Bacterial Pathogens
article.license: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are properly credited.

events.event_label: heat‑killed bacteria induce PTI‑like local resistance reducing HR
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

events.event_label: inoculation induces expression of PAL, CHS and CHI
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p010.s001: Early reports showed that some defence-related genes (PAL – phenylalanine-ammonia-lyase, CHS – chalcone synthase, CHI – chalcone isomerase) are activated after inoculation of Phaseolus vulgaris with non-pathogenic hrp mutants of Pseudomonas syringae pv. PMC4125134.p010.s002: syringae bacteria as well as with saprobic bacteria.

events.event_label: PIP upregulates PPP genes PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p090.s006: Interestingly, these 5 genes are the closer ones to phenylalanine, i.e. to the origin of the phenylpropanoid pathway, as can be seen in Figure 4. PMC4125134.p090.s007: Even PIP on its own caused a slight elevation in activities of the first 4 PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL), of which transcription of the C4H gene was most elevated (cca. 3 times).

events.event_label: PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I participate in phenylpropanoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p113.s001: Real time RT-PCR investigation of activity changes of the identified phenylpropanoid genes in response to P. s. syringae hrcC-, flg22 and their combinations with PIP (Figure 6C) showed elevation of gene activities of certain genes in the PIP-combination treatments, as compared to the corresponding elicitor alone-treatment. PMC4125134.p113.s002: These genes were exactly the first five investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), closest to the origin of the pathway. PMC4125134.p113.s003: This could be caused by a compensation feedback present in the plant leaves, which senses lack of C4H products in some way. PMC4125134.p113.s004: The first four investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL) were slightly activated upon treatment solely with PIP, and of the four, activation of C4H was highest, about 3-fold.

events.event_label: OMT and F5H participate in phenylpropanoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well. PMC4125134.p071.s005: Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL). PMC4125134.p071.s006: Activation of the genes encoding the enzymes of the next stages of the pathway, such as ortho-methyltransferases (OMT) and ferulate-5-hydroxylase (F5H) was also significant. PMC4125134.p071.s007: Three isoenzymes of the peroxidases that oxidize the aromatic alcoholic compounds into lignins were also activated.

events.event_label: AvrPto suppresses PTI and reduces callose
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p014.s006: tomato DC3000 to suppress activation of plant cell wall genes. PMC4125134.p014.s007: Hauck et al. found that AvrPto is an effector that suppresses cell-wall based defense in PTI. PMC4125134.p014.s008: This was supported by reduced callose staining - an indicator of papilla formation - in Avr Pto transgenic Arabidopsis plants.

events.event_label: PAL, C4H and 4CL participate in phenylpropanoid pathway

PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well. PMC4125134.p071.s005: Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL). PMC4125134.p071.s006: Activation of the genes encoding the enzymes of the next stages of the pathway, such as ortho-methyltransferases (OMT) and ferulate-5-hydroxylase (F5H) was also significant. PMC4125134.p071.s007: Three isoenzymes of the peroxidases that oxidize the aromatic alcoholic compounds into lignins were also activated.

events.event_label: C90 receptor‑kinase and MAP‑kinases C24/C58 participate in MAPK pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p066.s001: Signal transduction-related genes were selected for further transcriptional investigation as this group of genes might mean a key to the regulation of the development and action of PTI. PMC4125134.p066.s002: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs. PMC4125134.p066.s003: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58). PMC4125134.p066.s004: Interestingly, contigs of genes representing three calcium-dependent proteins were also found (C53 - calcium-dependent protein kinase 4, C95 - Avr9/Cf-9 rapidly elicited protein 31, C125 - annexin-like protein).

events.event_label: PIP inhibits C4H activity in phenylpropanoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p076.s001: To assess if disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway causes a noticeable change in the efficiency of PTI we chose to inhibit C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s002: This enzyme, second just after PAL, catalyzes a rate limiting reaction, causing a bottleneck effect, so its inhibition will have an impact on all further reactions. PMC4125134.p076.s003: Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s004: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta .

events.event_label: papillae accumulate polyphenolics, callose and hydroxyproline‑rich extensins
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p014.s001: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. PMC4125134.p014.s002: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins. PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. PMC4125134.p014.s004: In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

events.event_label: Detection of cinnamic and p-coumaric acids in tobacco leaf tissue
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p085.s001: A) Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) separation and densitometric measurement of two phenolic compounds, cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid in PTI-induced and control tobacco leaf tissue. PMC4125134.p085.s002: Tobacco leaves were treated with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), piperonylic acid (PIP), P. syringae hrcC- bacteria (hrcC), their combination (PIP+hrcC), flagellin 22 peptide (flg22) and combination of piperonylic acid and flagellin 22 peptide (PIP+flg22). PMC4125134.p085.s003: Tissue samples (500 mg) were collected 6 hours after infiltration. PMC4125134.p085.s004: Separation and detection of p-coumaric acid and cinnamic acid was carried out by thin layer chromatography (TLC) and UV densitometry.

events.event_label: C4H converts cinnamic acid to p-coumaric acid
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p087.s001: p-Coumaric acid is directly synthetized from cinnamic acid in plants by C4H. PMC4125134.p087.s002: p-Coumaric acid (Figure 6A) was not detectable in samples inoculated only with DMSO or PIP (negative controls), as opposed to the other samples inoculated with either one of the PTI elicitors P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p087.s003: syringae hrcC- (3.7 nmol/g fresh weight) or flg22 peptide (2.5 nmol/g fresh weight). PMC4125134.p087.s004: However, when PIP was added to the elicitors, concentration of p-coumarate was measured to be less than half at 6 hpi.

events.event_label: Chalcone synthase and chalcone isomerase are associated with PTI
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p093.s001: Tobacco is a well-established model plant for plant-microbe interaction studies, especially in the field of plant-bacterial interactions and a good model plant of the Solanaceae family. PMC4125134.p093.s002: Plant science results should not be generalized incautiously, as differences between plant-groups always occur. PMC4125134.p093.s003: For example in the study of Jakobek and co-workers , bean was used as a model plant and chalcone synthase and chalcone isomerase as indicator genes of PTI, besides a PAL and a chitinase gene. PMC4125134.p093.s004: However the first two indicator genes seem to be specific for leguminous PTI, as they are activated in Medicago as well but not in Arabidopsis and tobacco (Szatmari et al. unpublished).

PMC4125134.p094.s006: So the suppression subtractive hybridization method in our hands resulted in a set of useful data comparable to that generated by microarray experiments. PMC4125134.p094.s007: We have to mention that our dataset might include some wounding-related genes, as non-treated control leaves were used for subtraction initially. PMC4125134.p094.s008: The interplay between PAMP- and DAMP- (Damage Associated Molecular Pattern) induced signaling and defence mechanisms has been characterized, and some common and also some distinct features have been found. PMC4125134.p094.s009: For example flg22 and OG (oligogalacturonides, typical example of DAMP) induced overlapping early transcriptional changes in Arabidopsis, but the number of activated genes and the amplitude of these changes was significantly higher after flg22 treatment than after OG treatment. PMC4125134.p094.s010: Late responses were on the other hand largely different between the two elicitor types.

events.event_label: LPS and avr trigger Ca2+ influx
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p099.s003: De Torres et al. showed that a sustained elevation of plant intracellular Ca2+ levels occurs in an apparently avr-dependent manner. PMC4125134.p099.s004: Earlier work from the same laboratory however showed the existence of an early peak of intracellular Ca2+ level, which was triggered by bacteria mutant in the type III secretion system (PTI-inducing) as well as incompatible strains. PMC4125134.p099.s005: This common early peak could possibly be related to onset of PTI. PMC4125134.p099.s006: Ali et al. found that the MAMP LPS, just like avr gene products is capable of generating Ca2+-influx into plant cells. PMC4125134.p099.s007: Moreover Aslam et al. showed that the MAMPs flg22 and elf18 cause an increase in plant intracellular Ca2+ levels 1–4 minutes after treatment.

events.event_label: flg22 and elf18 increase intracellular Ca2+ levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p099.s006: Ali et al. found that the MAMP LPS, just like avr gene products is capable of generating Ca2+-influx into plant cells. PMC4125134.p099.s007: Moreover Aslam et al. showed that the MAMPs flg22 and elf18 cause an increase in plant intracellular Ca2+ levels 1–4 minutes after treatment.

events.event_label: PIP inhibits C4H activity and PTI development
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p104.s001: Inhibition of C4H activity and PTI development by PIP

events.event_label: PIP inhibition of C4H reduces PTI
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p107.s001: Our HR-inhibition test results indicated that a significant decrease in the HR-inhibiting ability of PTI-inducing bacteria occurred (Figure 5A, 5B). PMC4125134.p107.s002: This means that inhibition of C4H by PIP inhibited PTI as well to a certain extent, determined by our experiments. PMC4125134.p107.s003: Thus, disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway at an early stage had an adverse effect on the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p107.s004: Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.

events.event_label: flg22 peptide activates PTI in Arabidopsis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p094.s001: Several authors ( ) studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis model plants. PMC4125134.p094.s002: Although exact parameters (bacterium strains, times of sampling) are different, the numbers of activated genes are comparable. PMC4125134.p094.s003: Treatments inducing PTI in planta, such as injection of flg22 peptide or infection with P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p094.s004: tomato DC3000 hrpA resulted in activation of 220–250 genes within 0.5–7 hours according to the above authors.

events.event_label: PIP inhibits C4H leading to cinnamate accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p112.s001: The basic level of cinnamate increased when PIP was applied. PMC4125134.p112.s002: This could mean that a flow of cinnamate towards another compound(s) is blocked by PIP, therefore cinnamate accumulated. PMC4125134.p112.s003: We know that PIP is a specific inhibitor of C4H therefore we expected this result. PMC4125134.p112.s004: Also, coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples were reduced when PIP was co-infiltrated with either of the two used elicitors.

events.event_label: heat‑killed pathogenic bacteria induce local resistance in tobacco leaves and suppress hypersensitive response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

PMC4125134.p071.s001: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library. PMC4125134.p071.s002: Each PTI-activated PPP gene is indicated with coloured letters in Figure 4, showing that nearly all of the enzymatic steps leading to lignification are represented. PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well.

events.event_label: PTI activates phenylpropanoid genes involved in cell‑wall formation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p101.s001: Genes related to formation of cell wall deposits were highly represented in our PTI-related cDNA library. PMC4125134.p101.s002: These were genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway and also some genes of glycine-rich and proline-rich proteins. PMC4125134.p101.s003: Our PTI-activated phenylpropanoid genes covered a major part of the phenylpropanoid pathway (Figure 4). PMC4125134.p101.s004: Real time RT-PCR confirmed both early (6 hpi) and late (48 hpi) activation of these genes (Figure 3B).

events.event_label: PAL produces cinnamate; C4H converts cinnamate to p‑coumarate and lowers cinnamate
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p111.s006: However, in the case of flg22 peptide treatment only the expected increase in p-coumarate level was observed, while the level of cinnamic acid did not change in these 6 hpi samples as compared to the DMSO control. PMC4125134.p111.s007: A possible explanation to this phenomenon could be that a dynamic equilibrium state of the cinnamate level was maintained here, which would mean that the PAL enzyme could keep up production of cinnamate from phenylalanine at the rate of C4H converting cinnamate to p-coumarate. PMC4125134.p111.s008: In the case of P. syringae hrcC- bacteria a higher level of C4H induction might have occurred, with which PAL could not keep up completely, therefore a relative decrease in cinnamate occurred. PMC4125134.p111.s009: We do not know however if there are other enzymes involved in converting cinnamate into different derivatives, for example into salicylic acid. PMC4125134.p111.s010: Therefore the exact enzymatic mechanisms creating the measured changes in the two phenolic acids will require more studies, for example a higher scale metabolic profiling experiment, preferably including a time series with earlier time points than the 6 hpi samples used here, and/or also applying higher concentrations of flg22.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p113.s003: This could be caused by a compensation feedback present in the plant leaves, which senses lack of C4H products in some way. events.event_label: PTI induces PAL and PIP inhibits C4H, causing cinnamate accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p086.s006: Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant. PMC4125134.p086.s007: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H. PMC4125134.p086.s008: This transformation is then stuck by the inhibitor PIP, resulting in high levels of cinnamate.

events.event_label: Flagellin perception via FLS2 activates MAPK cascade
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p098.s001: Unraveling the signaling pathways involved in PTI would bring us closer to the possibility of breeding or engineering plants with a higher capacity of PTI. PMC4125134.p098.s002: Some authors have already made important steps towards this task. PMC4125134.p098.s003: Asai et al. followed a complete MAPK cascade in Arabidopsis from the perception of the MAMP flagellin by its receptor-like-kinase-type receptor FLS2 to the corresponding transcription factors. PMC4125134.p098.s004: However, genetic evidence later showed that the first element, MEKK1 in fact belongs to another module: MEKK1-MKK1-MPK4.

events.event_label: hrp gene activity modulates HR and PTI
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p017.s001: Hrp/hrc mutants of the Pseudomonas syringae pv. syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off. PMC4125134.p017.s002: The hrcC mutant is especially useful for investigating PTI, as its ability to induce HR in tobacco is completely abolished, while the induced PTI can nicely be detected by an HR-inhibition test, and it is also a potent elicitor of PTI-related genes.

events.event_label: C4H produces p‑Coumaric acid; DMSO and PIP reduce its accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p087.s001: p-Coumaric acid is directly synthetized from cinnamic acid in plants by C4H. PMC4125134.p087.s002: p-Coumaric acid (Figure 6A) was not detectable in samples inoculated only with DMSO or PIP (negative controls), as opposed to the other samples inoculated with either one of the PTI elicitors P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p087.s003: syringae hrcC- (3.7 nmol/g fresh weight) or flg22 peptide (2.5 nmol/g fresh weight). PMC4125134.p087.s004: However, when PIP was added to the elicitors, concentration of p-coumarate was measured to be less than half at 6 hpi.

events.event_label: PIP upregulates multiple phenylpropanoid pathway genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p090.s003: Values in Figure 6B were normalized against actin and water treated control, like in the other RT-PCR measurements. PMC4125134.p090.s004: In general, we saw again that P. s. hrcC- caused stronger activation than flg22. PMC4125134.p090.s005: In the case of 5 out of 7 phenylpropanoid genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), addition of PIP to the PTI elicitors caused a slight elevation in gene activity. PMC4125134.p090.s006: Interestingly, these 5 genes are the closer ones to phenylalanine, i.e. to the origin of the phenylpropanoid pathway, as can be seen in Figure 4. PMC4125134.p090.s007: Even PIP on its own caused a slight elevation in activities of the first 4 PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL), of which transcription of the C4H gene was most elevated (cca. 3 times).

events.event_label: DMSO and PIP reduce p-coumaric acid levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p087.s001: p-Coumaric acid is directly synthetized from cinnamic acid in plants by C4H. PMC4125134.p087.s002: p-Coumaric acid (Figure 6A) was not detectable in samples inoculated only with DMSO or PIP (negative controls), as opposed to the other samples inoculated with either one of the PTI elicitors P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p087.s003: syringae hrcC- (3.7 nmol/g fresh weight) or flg22 peptide (2.5 nmol/g fresh weight). PMC4125134.p087.s004: However, when PIP was added to the elicitors, concentration of p-coumarate was measured to be less than half at 6 hpi.

events.event_label: PAL produces cinnamate; C4H converts cinnamate to p-coumarate
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p111.s006: However, in the case of flg22 peptide treatment only the expected increase in p-coumarate level was observed, while the level of cinnamic acid did not change in these 6 hpi samples as compared to the DMSO control. PMC4125134.p111.s007: A possible explanation to this phenomenon could be that a dynamic equilibrium state of the cinnamate level was maintained here, which would mean that the PAL enzyme could keep up production of cinnamate from phenylalanine at the rate of C4H converting cinnamate to p-coumarate. PMC4125134.p111.s008: In the case of P. syringae hrcC- bacteria a higher level of C4H induction might have occurred, with which PAL could not keep up completely, therefore a relative decrease in cinnamate occurred. PMC4125134.p111.s009: We do not know however if there are other enzymes involved in converting cinnamate into different derivatives, for example into salicylic acid. PMC4125134.p111.s010: Therefore the exact enzymatic mechanisms creating the measured changes in the two phenolic acids will require more studies, for example a higher scale metabolic profiling experiment, preferably including a time series with earlier time points than the 6 hpi samples used here, and/or also applying higher concentrations of flg22.

events.event_label: SA does not affect PTI and inoculation reduces SA levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p103.s001: It was interesting that genes of reactions leading to lignin formation were overrepresented while branches to salicylic acid, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and stilbenes were not present at all (Figure 4). PMC4125134.p103.s002: This was not unexpected based on our and others' findings that salicylic acid (SA) is not an important factor of local PTI in tobacco. PMC4125134.p103.s003: The innovative work of Huang et al. showed the lack of SA accumulation after inoculation of tobacco with P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p103.s004: tomato DC3000 hrcC− mutant using a SA biosensor.

events.event_label: PTI alters phenylpropanoid pathway metabolites and enzymes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p084.s001: Changes in cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid metabolite levels, and transcript levels of the related PPP enzymes in PTI-induced, PIP inhibitor-treated, and control tobacco leaf tissue.

events.event_label: Heat‑killed bacteria increase coumaric acid, reduced by PIP
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p088.s001: TLC experiments with heat-killed P. s. hrcC and heat-killed P. s. hrcC+PIP gave similar results to those with live P. s. hrcC or with the flg22 peptide. PMC4125134.p088.s002: That is, coumaric acid was formed in heat-killed P. s. hrcC treated samples, and much less of it was detected when PIP was also added (i.e. in heat-killed P. s. hrcC+PIP treated samples).

events.event_label: PIP inhibition of C4H reduces p-coumarate levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p083.s001: Thin layer chromatography (TLC) was applied to measure the levels of cinnamic acid, the second phenolic acid in the phenylpropanoid pathway and precursor of p-coumaric acid, moreover substrate of the key rate limiting enzyme of the pathway: C4H (Figure 6A). PMC4125134.p083.s002: Levels of the product, p-coumarate were also measured in tobacco leaf tissue in response to treatments with PTI inducing bacteria (P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p083.s003: syringae hrcC, 108 CFU/ml) and the elicitor flagellin peptide (Flg22, 1 µM), and the C4H inhibitor PIP (1 mM).

PMC4125134.p017.s001: Hrp/hrc mutants of the Pseudomonas syringae pv. syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off. PMC4125134.p017.s002: The hrcC mutant is especially useful for investigating PTI, as its ability to induce HR in tobacco is completely abolished, while the induced PTI can nicely be detected by an HR-inhibition test, and it is also a potent elicitor of PTI-related genes.

PMC4125134.p071.s001: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library. PMC4125134.p071.s002: Each PTI-activated PPP gene is indicated with coloured letters in Figure 4, showing that nearly all of the enzymatic steps leading to lignification are represented. PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well.

PMC4125134.p084.s001: Changes in cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid metabolite levels, and transcript levels of the related PPP enzymes in PTI-induced, PIP inhibitor-treated, and control tobacco leaf tissue.

events.event_label: cinnamic acid is a substrate in the phenylpropanoid pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p083.s001: Thin layer chromatography (TLC) was applied to measure the levels of cinnamic acid, the second phenolic acid in the phenylpropanoid pathway and precursor of p-coumaric acid, moreover substrate of the key rate limiting enzyme of the pathway: C4H (Figure 6A). PMC4125134.p083.s002: Levels of the product, p-coumarate were also measured in tobacco leaf tissue in response to treatments with PTI inducing bacteria (P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p083.s003: syringae hrcC, 108 CFU/ml) and the elicitor flagellin peptide (Flg22, 1 µM), and the C4H inhibitor PIP (1 mM).

PMC4125134.p113.s001: Real time RT-PCR investigation of activity changes of the identified phenylpropanoid genes in response to P. s. syringae hrcC-, flg22 and their combinations with PIP (Figure 6C) showed elevation of gene activities of certain genes in the PIP-combination treatments, as compared to the corresponding elicitor alone-treatment. PMC4125134.p113.s002: These genes were exactly the first five investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), closest to the origin of the pathway. PMC4125134.p113.s003: This could be caused by a compensation feedback present in the plant leaves, which senses lack of C4H products in some way. PMC4125134.p113.s004: The first four investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL) were slightly activated upon treatment solely with PIP, and of the four, activation of C4H was highest, about 3-fold. PMC4125134.p113.s005: This might again be result of a feedback loop sensing C4H activity itself, or p-coumarate, or any downstream products. PMC4125134.p113.s006: The actual mechanisms behind these findings require further investigations.

PMC4125134.p090.s003: Values in Figure 6B were normalized against actin and water treated control, like in the other RT-PCR measurements. PMC4125134.p090.s004: In general, we saw again that P. s. hrcC- caused stronger activation than flg22. PMC4125134.p090.s005: In the case of 5 out of 7 phenylpropanoid genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), addition of PIP to the PTI elicitors caused a slight elevation in gene activity. PMC4125134.p090.s006: Interestingly, these 5 genes are the closer ones to phenylalanine, i.e. to the origin of the phenylpropanoid pathway, as can be seen in Figure 4. PMC4125134.p090.s007: Even PIP on its own caused a slight elevation in activities of the first 4 PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL), of which transcription of the C4H gene was most elevated (cca. 3 times).

events.event_label: Heat‑killed bacteria induce local resistance and suppress hypersensitive response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

PMC4125134.p099.s001: Another group of our activated signaling genes during PTI were related to Ca2+ mediated signaling. PMC4125134.p099.s002: Only a few examples of MAMP-induced elevation of intracellular Ca2+ levels exist in plants so far. PMC4125134.p099.s003: De Torres et al. showed that a sustained elevation of plant intracellular Ca2+ levels occurs in an apparently avr-dependent manner. PMC4125134.p099.s004: Earlier work from the same laboratory however showed the existence of an early peak of intracellular Ca2+ level, which was triggered by bacteria mutant in the type III secretion system (PTI-inducing) as well as incompatible strains. PMC4125134.p099.s005: This common early peak could possibly be related to onset of PTI. PMC4125134.p099.s006: Ali et al. found that the MAMP LPS, just like avr gene products is capable of generating Ca2+-influx into plant cells. PMC4125134.p099.s007: Moreover Aslam et al. showed that the MAMPs flg22 and elf18 cause an increase in plant intracellular Ca2+ levels 1–4 minutes after treatment.

events.event_label: PIP inhibition of C4H alters cinnamate and coumarate levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p112.s001: The basic level of cinnamate increased when PIP was applied. PMC4125134.p112.s002: This could mean that a flow of cinnamate towards another compound(s) is blocked by PIP, therefore cinnamate accumulated. PMC4125134.p112.s003: We know that PIP is a specific inhibitor of C4H therefore we expected this result. PMC4125134.p112.s004: Also, coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples were reduced when PIP was co-infiltrated with either of the two used elicitors. PMC4125134.p112.s005: We can say that these TLC derived data correlated well with data from HR-inhibition experiments (Figure 5).

events.event_label: Hydroxyproline‑rich extensins accumulate in papillae and participate in cell‑wall fortification
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p014.s001: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. PMC4125134.p014.s002: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins. PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. PMC4125134.p014.s004: In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

events.event_label: PIP inhibition of C4H leads to accumulation of cinnamate
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p086.s006: Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant. PMC4125134.p086.s007: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H. PMC4125134.p086.s008: This transformation is then stuck by the inhibitor PIP, resulting in high levels of cinnamate.

PMC4125134.p111.s006: However, in the case of flg22 peptide treatment only the expected increase in p-coumarate level was observed, while the level of cinnamic acid did not change in these 6 hpi samples as compared to the DMSO control. PMC4125134.p111.s007: A possible explanation to this phenomenon could be that a dynamic equilibrium state of the cinnamate level was maintained here, which would mean that the PAL enzyme could keep up production of cinnamate from phenylalanine at the rate of C4H converting cinnamate to p-coumarate. PMC4125134.p111.s008: In the case of P. syringae hrcC- bacteria a higher level of C4H induction might have occurred, with which PAL could not keep up completely, therefore a relative decrease in cinnamate occurred. PMC4125134.p111.s009: We do not know however if there are other enzymes involved in converting cinnamate into different derivatives, for example into salicylic acid. PMC4125134.p111.s010: Therefore the exact enzymatic mechanisms creating the measured changes in the two phenolic acids will require more studies, for example a higher scale metabolic profiling experiment, preferably including a time series with earlier time points than the 6 hpi samples used here, and/or also applying higher concentrations of flg22.

PMC4125134.p076.s001: To assess if disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway causes a noticeable change in the efficiency of PTI we chose to inhibit C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s002: This enzyme, second just after PAL, catalyzes a rate limiting reaction, causing a bottleneck effect, so its inhibition will have an impact on all further reactions. PMC4125134.p076.s003: Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s004: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta . PMC4125134.p076.s009: Only when the incompatible P. syringae pv. syringae 61 was co-infiltrated with PIP (i.e. 0 hpi) did we detect a lack of HR, possibly because of inhibition of bacterial activity by PIP. PMC4125134.p076.s010: Therefore after considering several possible C4H inhibitors from literature, PIP seemed to be the most suitable for our experiments because of its selective and quasi-irreversible nature.

PMC4125134.p113.s003: This could be caused by a compensation feedback present in the plant leaves, which senses lack of C4H products in some way. PMC4125134.p113.s004: The first four investigated PPP genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL) were slightly activated upon treatment solely with PIP, and of the four, activation of C4H was highest, about 3-fold. PMC4125134.p113.s005: This might again be result of a feedback loop sensing C4H activity itself, or p-coumarate, or any downstream products. PMC4125134.p113.s006: The actual mechanisms behind these findings require further investigations.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p090.s003: Values in Figure 6B were normalized against actin and water treated control, like in the other RT-PCR measurements. PMC4125134.p090.s005: In the case of 5 out of 7 phenylpropanoid genes (PALa, PALb, C4H, 4CL, OMT-I), addition of PIP to the PTI elicitors caused a slight elevation in gene activity. events.event_label: PIP inhibition of C4H increases cinnamate levels
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p086.s001: Cinnamic acid was present at a basic level of cca. PMC4125134.p086.s002: 11 nmol/g fresh weight in our samples, as seen in DMSO controls (Figure 6A). PMC4125134.p086.s003: This level was generally elevated upon any type of treatment with the C4H inhibitor PIP, either alone or combined with the PTI elicitors. PMC4125134.p086.s004: Elevation compared to control (DMSO) treatment was not significant in the case of PIP treatment alone, but it was definitely significant when PIP was combined with either PTI elicitor. PMC4125134.p086.s006: Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant. PMC4125134.p086.s007: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H. PMC4125134.p086.s008: This transformation is then stuck by the inhibitor PIP, resulting in high levels of cinnamate.

PMC4125134.p001.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) in Tobacco: Isolation of Activated Genes Suggests Role of the Phenylpropanoid Pathway in Inhibition of Bacterial Pathogens

PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

PMC4125134.p005.s003: Subtraction Suppressive Hybridization (SSH) resulted in 156 PTI-activated genes. PMC4125134.p005.s004: A cDNA microarray was generated from the SSH clone library.

PMC4125134.p009.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens. PMC4125134.p009.s002: PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level. PMC4125134.p009.s003: Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen. PMC4125134.p009.s004: This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

PMC4125134.p011.s001: Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis. PMC4125134.p011.s002: Navarro et al. treated plants with a fragment of bacterial flagellin (flg22 peptide) and found that the flagellin rapidly elicited (FLARE) genes at 30 and 60 hpi are mainly activated, and only a few are repressed. PMC4125134.p011.s003: Activation is thought to be achieved by derepression, rather than stimulation. PMC4125134.p011.s004: De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical.

PMC4125134.p012.s001: Identification of its elicitors was an important step in the studying of PTI. PMC4125134.p012.s002: These so called MAMPs (microbe-associated molecular patterns) are conserved molecules that are indispensable constituents of the bacterial cell. PMC4125134.p012.s003: They include flagellin, LPS (lipopolysaccharide,), cold shock protein and elongation factor Tu. PMC4125134.p012.s004: Identification of the flagellin receptor FLS2 opened the way to determination of downstream signaling events.

PMC4125134.p013.s001: Most studies about plant PTI, including the ones mentioned above concentrated on early events of PTI, specialized in deciphering the events of signal perception and transduction, identifying elicitors, their receptors and the downstream signaling events. PMC4125134.p013.s002: In contrast there is very little information on the metabolic alterations that eventually protect the plant after PTI was triggered, and hardly anything is known about the mechanisms leading to inhibition of bacterial activity.

PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. PMC4125134.p014.s004: In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell. PMC4125134.p014.s005: The possible importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation in PTI is supported by transcriptional evidence that showed the ability of the compatible Arabidopsis pathogen Pseudomonas syringae pv. PMC4125134.p014.s006: tomato DC3000 to suppress activation of plant cell wall genes. PMC4125134.p014.s007: Hauck et al. found that AvrPto is an effector that suppresses cell-wall based defense in PTI.

PMC4125134.p015.s001: The phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathway (PPP) could promote cell wall fortification by lignin synthesis, but it is also an important source of compounds with direct antimicrobial activities. PMC4125134.p015.s002: It is not known, whether it plays a role in PTI against bacteria, but its importance in different plant-fungal interactions has long been shown. PMC4125134.p015.s003: Tobacco plants with suppressed PAL activity - the second enzyme of PPP - have been shown to be more susceptible to the compatible pathogen Cercospora nicotianae .

PMC4125134.p018.s003: At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH). PMC4125134.p018.s004: Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR. PMC4125134.p018.s005: A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.

PMC4125134.p062.s001: We used MAPMAN (http://mapman.gabipd.org) to visualize the differences between early (6 h) and late (48 h) sets of activated, biotic stress-related genes during PTI. PMC4125134.p062.s002: Potato TC-s (Tentative Contigs) could be assigned to 158 PTI-related tobacco contigs based on similarity. PMC4125134.p062.s003: Thirty-five of these tobacco contigs were mapped on the biotic stress pathway. PMC4125134.p062.s004: The activation data from our microarray hybridizations were then visualized by the potato biotic stress pathway in MAPMAN created by Rotter and coworkers (Information S2).

PMC4125134.p067.s002: Altogether, 11 out of 16 contigs representing various signaling-related genes were significantly activated according to real time RT-PCR measurements.

PMC4125134.p075.s001: Inhibition of C4H activity decreased the effectiveness of PTI

PMC4125134.p076.s003: Piperonilic acid (PIP) was chosen, known to be a selective, quasi-irreversible inhibitor of C4H. PMC4125134.p076.s004: Although PIP is toxic to bacteria to a certain extent, it is metabolized fast in planta . PMC4125134.p076.s005: Our in vitro co-incubation experiments showed that proliferation of P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p076.s006: syringae 61 incubated in 1 mM PIP solution for 30 minutes was lowered by two orders of magnitude. PMC4125134.p076.s007: In a dilution series of PIP, 50 µM was the first concentration not to influence bacterial proliferation (data not shown).

PMC4125134.p076.s006: syringae 61 incubated in 1 mM PIP solution for 30 minutes was lowered by two orders of magnitude. PMC4125134.p076.s007: In a dilution series of PIP, 50 µM was the first concentration not to influence bacterial proliferation (data not shown). PMC4125134.p076.s008: We tested if these characteristics could affect our planned experiments and found that it had no adverse effects on HR-development if bacteria were injected at least 2 hours after PIP administration (Information S4 A). PMC4125134.p076.s009: Only when the incompatible P. syringae pv. syringae 61 was co-infiltrated with PIP (i.e. 0 hpi) did we detect a lack of HR, possibly because of inhibition of bacterial activity by PIP. PMC4125134.p076.s010: Therefore after considering several possible C4H inhibitors from literature, PIP seemed to be the most suitable for our experiments because of its selective and quasi-irreversible nature.

PMC4125134.p080.s007: Conductivity (corresponding to electrolyte leakage and HR) was higher both in the 5-hour and the 6-hour P. s. hrcC+PIP samples.

PMC4125134.p081.s001: To rule out the possibility that a direct inhibitory action of PIP on the P. syringae pv. syringae hrcC bacteria used for pretreatment caused the detected difference in the extent of PTI development, we repeated the HR-inhibition experiment with heat-killed P. s. hrcC and heat-killed P. s. hrcC+PIP as pretreatments. PMC4125134.p081.s002: Results were similar with heat-killed as with live P. s. hrcC pretreatments, i.e. addition of PIP caused diminished HR upon challenge inoculation with incompatible strain P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p081.s003: syringae 61 (representative leaf image: Information S4 B).
PMC4125134.p082.s001: Production of p-coumarate is strongly induced by PTI elicitors, and is inhibited by PIP

PMC4125134.p083.s001: Thin layer chromatography (TLC) was applied to measure the levels of cinnamic acid, the second phenolic acid in the phenylpropanoid pathway and precursor of p-coumaric acid, moreover substrate of the key rate limiting enzyme of the pathway: C4H (Figure 6A). PMC4125134.p083.s002: Levels of the product, p-coumarate were also measured in tobacco leaf tissue in response to treatments with PTI inducing bacteria (P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p083.s003: syringae hrcC, 108 CFU/ml) and the elicitor flagellin peptide (Flg22, 1 µM), and the C4H inhibitor PIP (1 mM).

PMC4125134.p089.s003: We would have liked to see how changes in transcriptional levels relate to changes in metabolite levels in Figure 6A. PMC4125134.p089.s004: We expected that if PIP is supposed to inhibit PTI through inhibiting C4H, than the plant might try to counteract its effect on a transcriptional level. PMC4125134.p089.s005: This would mean that PIP addition would elevate transcriptional levels of some PTI-related PPP genes, or at least of C4H.

PMC4125134.p089.s003: We would have liked to see how changes in transcriptional levels relate to changes in metabolite levels in Figure 6A. PMC4125134.p089.s004: We expected that if PIP is supposed to inhibit PTI through inhibiting C4H, than the plant might try to counteract its effect on a transcriptional level. PMC4125134.p089.s005: This would mean that PIP addition would elevate transcriptional levels of some PTI-related PPP genes, or at least of C4H.
PMC4125134.p092.s001: Suppression subtractive hybridization to isolate genes having a role in PTI

PMC4125134.p094.s003: Treatments inducing PTI in planta, such as injection of flg22 peptide or infection with P. syringae pv. PMC4125134.p094.s004: tomato DC3000 hrpA resulted in activation of 220–250 genes within 0.5–7 hours according to the above authors. PMC4125134.p094.s005: Our study identified 176 PTI-related tobacco genes assembled as contigs from 425 sequenced clones. PMC4125134.p094.s006: So the suppression subtractive hybridization method in our hands resulted in a set of useful data comparable to that generated by microarray experiments. PMC4125134.p094.s007: We have to mention that our dataset might include some wounding-related genes, as non-treated control leaves were used for subtraction initially.

PMC4125134.p099.s001: Another group of our activated signaling genes during PTI were related to Ca2+ mediated signaling. PMC4125134.p099.s002: Only a few examples of MAMP-induced elevation of intracellular Ca2+ levels exist in plants so far. PMC4125134.p099.s003: De Torres et al. showed that a sustained elevation of plant intracellular Ca2+ levels occurs in an apparently avr-dependent manner. PMC4125134.p099.s004: Earlier work from the same laboratory however showed the existence of an early peak of intracellular Ca2+ level, which was triggered by bacteria mutant in the type III secretion system (PTI-inducing) as well as incompatible strains.

PMC4125134.p105.s001: We applied PIP, a potent selective inhibitor of C4H – which is an early rate-limiting enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway – to investigate if disturbing the pathway at a key step has any effect on the outcome of PTI induction (Figure 5).

PMC4125134.p106.s001: According to our experiments (Information S4) injected PIP seems to have a short inhibitory action on bacteria in planta, but paralelly it seems to have a longer effect on PTI development. PMC4125134.p106.s002: One possible explanation of this would be that PIP, as a quasi-irreversible inhibitor could permanently bind to the active sites of the available cinnamic-acid-4-hydroxylase (C4H) enzymes, thus blocking enzymatic activity for a longer period of time. PMC4125134.p106.s003: It is possible that only the excess free amount of PIP is metabolized soon afterwards. PMC4125134.p106.s004: However, this explanation is hypothetic and requires further studies.

PMC4125134.p107.s003: Thus, disturbing the phenylpropanoid pathway at an early stage had an adverse effect on the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p107.s004: Visual evaluation of the HR-inhibition tests was also supported by electrolyte leakage measurements, where elevations in conductivity are related to the degree of HR necrosis.

PMC4125134.p109.s001: Recently Chakravarthy et al used virus-induced gene silencing (VIGS) and a cell death-based assay similar to our HR-test to assess PTI in Nicotiana benthamiana. PMC4125134.p109.s002: Their cell death-based assay is very spectacular. PMC4125134.p109.s003: Interestingly, screening a tomato library of PAMP induced genes, they found VIGS of a C4H gene to compromise PTI in their experimental system. PMC4125134.p109.s004: This tomato C4H segment (GW691616) is 89% identical to the complete tobacco C4H CDS (DQ350353.1) corresponding to the C4H contig investigated in this manuscript.
PMC4125134.p110.s001: p-Coumarate formation is linked to PTI elicitation in tobacco

PMC4125134.p111.s001: To assess, if the actual flux of phenolic compounds follows our assumptions based on HR-inhibition tests and transcriptional data, we quantified cinnamate and p-coumarate, the second and third phenolic compounds in the phenylpropanoid pathway (as cinnamic and p-coumaric acids) by the use of a TLC-densitometry method (Figure 6A and B). PMC4125134.p111.s002: Transcriptional and HR-inhibition test data implied that C4H activity should be enhanced during PTI. PMC4125134.p111.s003: Therefore we expected a decrease in cinnamate and a parallel increase in p-coumarate levels in PTI-induced samples. PMC4125134.p111.s004: This exactly occurred in the case of treatment with PTI-inducing P. syringae pv.

PMC4125134.p114.s001: All in all, what our results strongly imply is that cinnamate is at least partly converted into p-coumarate during the course of PTI. PMC4125134.p114.s002: This even seems to correlate with the strength of PTI, based on our HR-inhibition tests and electrolyte-leakage experiments.

PMC4125134.p116.s001: Based on transcriptional activation, HR-inhibition tests, electrolyte leakage experiments, and TLC results we concluded that C4H seems to play an important role in the development of PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s002: This however raises a lot of further questions to be answered in the future. PMC4125134.p116.s003: We are planning to develop a HPLC based method to measure changes of phenolic compounds at a higher scale, to better understand the roles of these compounds in PTI. PMC4125134.p116.s004: We are especially curious about the changes in the levels of phenylalanine, salicylic acid, other phenolic acids and alcohols, flavonoids etc.

PMC4125134.p117.s001: Investigating the role of PPP in PTI, one shall remember that the presumably formed phenolic acids (i.e. coumarate, caffeate, ferulate) have been shown to have direct antimicrobial activity, which might also add to the efficiency of PTI. PMC4125134.p117.s002: Importance of the antioxidant activity of phenylpropanoid products has been shown by Kostyn et al. in the plant–fungus interaction context. PMC4125134.p117.s003: The detected transcriptional activation of flax phenylpropanoid genes in response to Fusarium infection was supported by metabolite profiling. PMC4125134.p117.s004: Elevated antioxidant status of infected plants was verified by measuring ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) scavenging activities of identified compounds.

relations.subject: inoculation

evidence.text: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.

relations.evidence_preview: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p003.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance. PMC4125134.p003.s002: Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation. PMC4125134.p003.s003: Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria. PMC4125134.p003.s004: Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI) or Basal Resistance (BR) is a potent, symptomless form of plant resistance.
Upon inoculation of a plant with non-pathogens or pathogenicity-mutant bacteria, the induced PTI will prevent bacterial proliferation.
Developed PTI is also able to protect the plant from disease or HR (Hypersensitive Response) after a challenging infection with pathogenic bacteria.
Our aim was to reveal those PTI-related genes of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) that could possibly play a role in the protection of the plant from disease.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.

relations.evidence_preview: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p009.s001: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens. PMC4125134.p009.s002: PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level. PMC4125134.p009.s003: Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen. PMC4125134.p009.s004: This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Pattern Triggered Immunity (PTI), also known as Basal Resistance (BR) is a general form of defense that plants use to maintain their integrity by detecting and eradicating almost any type of invading organism, including non-pathogens.
PTI is symptomless in most cases; however extensive changes occur at the molecular level.

Effector Triggered Immunity (ETI), also known as gene-for-gene resistance of plants, on the other hand requires detection of specific effector proteins of pathogens by plant R-gene products, which eventually leads to the hypersensitive reaction (HR) of plant cells and eradication of that specific pathogen.
This manuscript focuses on molecular changes in plant cells during PTI.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis.

relations.evidence_preview: Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p011.s001: Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis. PMC4125134.p011.s002: Navarro et al. treated plants with a fragment of bacterial flagellin (flg22 peptide) and found that the flagellin rapidly elicited (FLARE) genes at 30 and 60 hpi are mainly activated, and only a few are repressed. PMC4125134.p011.s003: Activation is thought to be achieved by derepression, rather than stimulation. PMC4125134.p011.s004: De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Several authors studied the transcriptomic changes during PTI, mostly in Arabidopsis.

Navarro et al. treated plants with a fragment of bacterial flagellin (flg22 peptide) and found that the flagellin rapidly elicited (FLARE) genes at 30 and 60 hpi are mainly activated, and only a few are repressed. Activation is thought to be achieved by derepression, rather than stimulation.

De Torres et al. have found that up to 2 hpi transcriptional changes of HR, PTI, and the compatible relationship are mostly identical. Truman et al. therefore sought differences of transcriptional patterns at later time points.
At 4 hpi the compatible and hrp mutant bacteria still caused similar changes.

relations.subject: MAMPs

evidence.text: They include flagellin, LPS (lipopolysaccharide,), cold shock protein and elongation factor Tu.

relations.evidence_preview: They include flagellin, LPS (lipopolysaccharide,), cold shock protein and elongation factor Tu.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p012.s001: Identification of its elicitors was an important step in the studying of PTI. PMC4125134.p012.s002: These so called MAMPs (microbe-associated molecular patterns) are conserved molecules that are indispensable constituents of the bacterial cell. PMC4125134.p012.s003: They include flagellin, LPS (lipopolysaccharide,), cold shock protein and elongation factor Tu. PMC4125134.p012.s004: Identification of the flagellin receptor FLS2 opened the way to determination of downstream signaling events.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Identification of its elicitors was an important step in the studying of PTI.
These so called MAMPs (microbe-associated molecular patterns) are conserved molecules that are indispensable constituents of the bacterial cell.
They include flagellin, LPS (lipopolysaccharide,), cold shock protein and elongation factor Tu.
Identification of the flagellin receptor FLS2 opened the way to determination of downstream signaling events.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Most studies about plant PTI, including the ones mentioned above concentrated on early events of PTI, specialized in deciphering the events of signal perception and transduction, identifying elicitors, their receptors and the downstream signaling events.

PMC4125134.p013.s002: In contrast there is very little information on the metabolic alterations that eventually protect the plant after PTI was triggered, and hardly anything is known about the mechanisms leading to inhibition of bacterial activity.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Most studies about plant PTI, including the ones mentioned above concentrated on early events of PTI, specialized in deciphering the events of signal perception and transduction, identifying elicitors, their receptors and the downstream signaling events.

In contrast there is very little information on the metabolic alterations that eventually protect the plant after PTI was triggered, and hardly anything is known about the mechanisms leading to inhibition of bacterial activity.

relations.subject: polyphenolics

evidence.text: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins.

relations.evidence_preview: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p014.s001: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. PMC4125134.p014.s002: Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins. PMC4125134.p014.s003: The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions. PMC4125134.p014.s004: In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Plant cell wall fortification is an important factor of mechanical resistance, the formed papillae are thought to constitute a barrier between the plant cell wall and invading microorganisms. Papillae are made up of polyphenolics, callose and a network of cell-wall fortifying glycoprotein matrix mainly consisting of glycin-rich proteins and hydroxyproline-rich extensins.
The importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation is well established in plant-fungal interactions.
In the case of bacteria this is speculated to result in a reduced accessibility of nutrients, plus a restricted ability to build up the TTSS and deliver effectors into the plant cell.

The possible importance of cell wall fortification and papilla formation in PTI is supported by transcriptional evidence that showed the ability of the compatible Arabidopsis pathogen Pseudomonas syringae pv.

relations.subject: PPP

evidence.text: The phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathway (PPP) could promote cell wall fortification by lignin synthesis, but it is also an important source of compounds with direct antimicrobial activities.

relations.evidence_preview: The phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathway (PPP) could promote cell wall fortification by lignin synthesis, but it is also an important source of compounds with direct antimicrobial activities.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p015.s001: The phenylpropanoid biosynthetic pathway (PPP) could promote cell wall fortification by lignin synthesis, but it is also an important source of compounds with direct antimicrobial activities. PMC4125134.p015.s002: It is not known, whether it plays a role in PTI against bacteria, but its importance in different plant-fungal interactions has long been shown. PMC4125134.p015.s003: Tobacco plants with suppressed PAL activity - the second enzyme of PPP - have been shown to be more susceptible to the compatible pathogen Cercospora nicotianae .

relations.triple: heat-killed pathogenic bacteria activates_or_induces local resistance
relations.subject: heat-killed pathogenic bacteria

evidence.text: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.

relations.evidence_preview: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p016.s001: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively. PMC4125134.p016.s002: Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS). PMC4125134.p016.s003: Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI. PMC4125134.p016.s004: We routinely use the HR-inhibiting effect of PTI as a macroscopical demonstration method.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Previous works from our laboratory showed that heat-killed pathogenic bacteria injected into tobacco leaves are able to induce a symptomless local resistance that protects the plant from a following compatible infection, or the hypersensitive response, when re-inoculated with compatible or incompatible pathogens respectively.
Later on we found that this local resistance can not only be induced by heat-killed pathogens, but also by saprobic bacteria and some hrp/hrc mutants; i.
e. pathogens mutated in their hypersensitivity and pathogenicity genes, many of which code for elements of the Type III Protein Secretion System (T3SS).
Based on our previous findings we regard the local resistance of tobacco as a manifestation of PTI.

relations.subject: hrp gene

evidence.text: Hrp/hrc mutants of the Pseudomonas syringae pv. syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off.

syringae 61 bacterial strain (original wild type causing brown spot on bean and HR on tobacco) are damaged in their ability to cause disease on bean or HR on tobacco to varying extent, depending on which hrp gene is turned off.

relations.subject: phenylpropanoid genes

evidence.text: A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.

relations.evidence_preview: A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.

PMC4125134.p018.s004: Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR. PMC4125134.p018.s005: A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.

evidence_context_sentences.text: At first we identified over 400 EST-s representing genes that are activated during PTI using suppression subtractive hybridization (SSH).
Following assembly of the EST sequences into 176 contigs, their transcriptional activation was confirmed by cDNA microarray and real time RT-PCR.

A highly represented group in our library, that of the phenylpropanoid genes, was strongly implicated to play an important role in mediating the antimicrobial effects of PTI, as shown by transcriptional (microarray and RT-PCR) data, as well as HR-inhibition assays using pharmacological inhibition of a key enzyme of the phenylpropanoid pathway.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: We used MAPMAN (http://mapman.gabipd.org) to visualize the differences between early (6 h) and late (48 h) sets of activated, biotic stress-related genes during PTI.

relations.evidence_preview: We used MAPMAN (http://mapman.gabipd.org) to visualize the differences between early (6 h) and late (48 h) sets of activated, biotic stress-related genes during PTI.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p062.s001: We used MAPMAN (http://mapman.gabipd.org) to visualize the differences between early (6 h) and late (48 h) sets of activated, biotic stress-related genes during PTI. PMC4125134.p062.s002: Potato TC-s (Tentative Contigs) could be assigned to 158 PTI-related tobacco contigs based on similarity. PMC4125134.p062.s003: Thirty-five of these tobacco contigs were mapped on the biotic stress pathway. PMC4125134.p062.s004: The activation data from our microarray hybridizations were then visualized by the potato biotic stress pathway in MAPMAN created by Rotter and coworkers (Information S2).

relations.subject: Signal transduction-related genes

evidence.text: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs.

relations.evidence_preview: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p066.s001: Signal transduction-related genes were selected for further transcriptional investigation as this group of genes might mean a key to the regulation of the development and action of PTI. PMC4125134.p066.s002: The group was highly represented among the identified PTI-related genes, 20 of 176 contigs, i.e. 11% of the contigs. PMC4125134.p066.s003: Members of the best described signal transduction pathway of plant PTI, the MAPK pathway were found: a receptor-kinase (C90) and two MAP-kinases (C24, C58). PMC4125134.p066.s004: Interestingly, contigs of genes representing three calcium-dependent proteins were also found (C53 - calcium-dependent protein kinase 4, C95 - Avr9/Cf-9 rapidly elicited protein 31, C125 - annexin-like protein).

relations.subject: lignin-type polyphenols

evidence.text: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library.

relations.evidence_preview: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p071.s001: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library. PMC4125134.p071.s002: Each PTI-activated PPP gene is indicated with coloured letters in Figure 4, showing that nearly all of the enzymatic steps leading to lignification are represented. PMC4125134.p071.s003: Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3). PMC4125134.p071.s004: All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Interestingly, a complete network of the phenylpropanoid pathway (PPP) genes leading towards synthesis of lignin-type polyphenols was found in our PTI-related subtracted library.

Each PTI-activated PPP gene is indicated with coloured letters in Figure 4, showing that nearly all of the enzymatic steps leading to lignification are represented. Real-time RT-PCR measurements confirmed that all of these genes are significantly activated during PTI (Figure 3B and Information S3).
All of the measured gene activities were significantly elevated at 6 hpi, and remained above baseline to different extent at 48 hpi as well.
Activated genes included early genes of the phenylpropanoid pathway such as two phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) genes, a cinnamic acid 4-hydroxilase (C4H), and a 4-coumarate:CoA-ligase (4CL).

relations.subject: cinnamic acid

evidence.text: A) Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) separation and densitometric measurement of two phenolic compounds, cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid in PTI-induced and control tobacco leaf tissue.

relations.evidence_preview: A) Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) separation and densitometric measurement of two phenolic compounds, cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid in PTI-induced and control tobacco leaf tissue.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p085.s001: A) Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) separation and densitometric measurement of two phenolic compounds, cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid in PTI-induced and control tobacco leaf tissue. PMC4125134.p085.s002: Tobacco leaves were treated with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), piperonylic acid (PIP), P. syringae hrcC- bacteria (hrcC), their combination (PIP+hrcC), flagellin 22 peptide (flg22) and combination of piperonylic acid and flagellin 22 peptide (PIP+flg22). PMC4125134.p085.s003: Tissue samples (500 mg) were collected 6 hours after infiltration. PMC4125134.p085.s004: Separation and detection of p-coumaric acid and cinnamic acid was carried out by thin layer chromatography (TLC) and UV densitometry.

evidence_context_sentences.text: A) Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) separation and densitometric measurement of two phenolic compounds, cinnamic acid and p-coumaric acid in PTI-induced and control tobacco leaf tissue.
Tobacco leaves were treated with dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), piperonylic acid (PIP), P.
syringae hrcC- bacteria (hrcC), their combination (PIP+hrcC), flagellin 22 peptide (flg22) and combination of piperonylic acid and flagellin 22 peptide (PIP+flg22).
Tissue samples (500 mg) were collected 6 hours after infiltration.

relations.subject: PTI

evidence.text: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H.

relations.evidence_preview: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4125134.p086.s006: Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant. PMC4125134.p086.s007: Knowing that PAL, the previous enzyme in the phenylpropanoid pathway is also activated upon PTI elicitor treatment, one might hypothesize that when elicitors are added, PAL creates elevated levels of cinnamate, which is then normally turned into coumarate by C4H. PMC4125134.p086.s008: This transformation is then stuck by the inhibitor PIP, resulting in high levels of cinnamate.

evidence_context_sentences.text: syringae pv.

syringae hrcC-) alone caused significant decrease in cinnamate levels (about halved the concentration) as expected, while treatment with flg22 peptide maintained cinnamate quantity at the basic level of 11 nmol/g fresh weight in the measured 6 hpi samples.

Looking at pairs of treatment X and treatment X+PIP, the largest difference was found between hrcC and hrcC+PIP treatment among our experimental settings, and difference between flg22 and flg22+PIP was also significant.
